tlos: Understand system functional decomposition.
Comprehend how functional architectures can help manage complexity.
Identify function vs. solution.
Understand functional control diagrams.

events.description: Teaches the fundamentals of Systems Engineering and how it applies to test. Covers complexity, systems theory, the scientific method, an abstract approach for decomposing the modern battlespace into test requirements, and a process for generating data and reducing system risk.
Builds on systems engineering modeling concepts and provides simple constructs for modeling systems from function to form.

events.eventName: Systems Engineering for Testers Project Written Report
events.description: The written report provides an opportunity for the student to engage individually in Systems Engineering artifact development and communicate their purpose and importance to complexity management, test strategy development, and risk management using a notional system provided by the instructor. This project consists of a short technical report on a notional complex system, providing an opportunity for the student to demonstrate comprehension across a spectrum of Systems Engineering artifacts.

tlos: The student will recall the core principles of human systems integration.

events.description: Introduces Human System Integration (HSI) as a systems-based scientific methodology and highlights test considerations. Presents considerations for the human element cross-over into all other disciplines. Introduces concepts of cognitive workload assessment and conducting HSI evaluations of systems, including qualitative and quantitative approaches.

Orients students to human systems testing in a simulation environment. During a 1.5-hour test block, each student will receive a simulator orientation and individual simulator tasks. Basic Performance Resource measures will be collected and used in appropriate curriculum events for analysis.

Reviews aspects of the human role in the operations process and describes the role of the TPS graduate in the Human Systems testing process. Emphasizes space/aviation Human Systems history as it relates to major aviation, military, and space developments. Covers control and display principles, cognitive workload assessment, basic functionality and limitations of human sensation and perception, components of situation awareness, models of decision making, and case studies.

Introduces the student to the science of Anthropometry (the measurement of human body dimensions). Anthropometric technicians will measure a variety of body dimensions for each student. Those dimensions will be placed into a Human Systems computer database which describes how students' individual body dimensions compare to the historic US Air Force aircrew population.

Exposes students to workload rating scales commonly used in test and evaluation. Students will receive an overview of each rating scale and guidance for use. Simple in-class exercises will reinforce the use of the rating scales. Students are expected to apply appropriate rating scales in later practical exercises.

Orients students to the scope and expectations of the Human Systems Evaluation Modified Technical Report and provides a dedicated opportunity for students to query the instructor regarding details of the report. Expectations for the system under test, data collection methods, and design of experiments guidelines will be provided.
Students will work in groups to collect task performance data, evaluate the system interface, and provide design change recommendations in the technical report.

events.eventName: Human Systems Evaluation Modified Technical Report
events.description: Students will work in groups to collect task performance data, evaluate the system interface, and provide design change recommendations in the technical report.

tlos: The student will recall core principles of test in acquisition.

events.description: Describes the types of information/services provided to battlespace managers and users by space assets, the primary characteristics of the space environment that impact the space asset's mission, space operations and associated capabilities, the four segments associated with space systems, how the acquisition/test approach is tailored by air breathing and vacuum breathing communities to manage acquisition/test risk, and specifics about the SSC Space Test Portfolio.

Students will conduct independent study using provided policy documents (DoD, DAF, and USSF instructions and guidance) related to Test and Evaluation. Students will review, research, and present relevant information to their classmates and instructors with a focus on their role in an Integrated Test Team (ITT). Each student will be assigned a single document to research and work with a group to develop a 45-minute oral presentation.

events.eventName: Test and Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP) Development
events.description: Students will be introduced to DAF and OSD guidance on development of test strategies and TEMPs. This course will consist of a lecture, guided research using OSD or DAF-provided tools, case studies, and a student project. Given actual acquisition program documents, students will review a TEMP from the perspective of an oversight organization.

tlos: The student will be able to negotiate complex systems modeling to support employing the Mission Risk Framework.
The student will characterize complex systems and create appropriate models.
The student will summarize the appropriate limits and utility of legacy risk management rubrics.
The student will infer the implications of human decision fallibility and its implications to modeling risks.

events.description: Introduces the framework which can be applied to complex systems and used for risk and safety analysis. This framework will be used throughout the curriculum for modeling and assessing a range of systems from simple to complex.

Provides the basic knowledge of test safety and hazard/risk identification, including safety planning, Final Safety Review (FSR), test plan approval, test execution, and the project test plan change process. Students receive initial Project Safety Lead (PSL) qualifications upon completion.

Provides the basic knowledge of test safety and hazard/risk identification, including safety planning, Final Safety Review (FSR), test plan approval, test execution, and the project test plan change process. Test safety processes will be reinforced through practical exercises throughout the course.
Provides the basic knowledge of test safety and hazard/risk identification through guest lecture. Test safety processes will be reinforced through practical exercises throughout the course.

Presents the Functional Mission Analysis, a hazard analysis technique based on an extended model of mishap causation. Component failures, unsafe interactions, and social-technical systems can be analyzed as a whole. Case studies of applications to past mishaps are presented. Students are led through applications to posed test projects to practice Functional Mission Analysis application.

tlos: The student will demonstrate the tenants of photo and safety chase.

events.eventName: Photo/Safety Chase Introduction and Familiarization MIB
events.description: Introduction and familiarization MIB for photo/safety chase missions. Students will learn differences in photo, safety, and area chase, and practice or observe roles and responsibilities for a variety of chase maneuvers.

Familiarizes the student with differences in photo, safety, and area chase. Students will practice or observe roles and responsibilities for a representative variety of chase maneuvers from both the test aircraft and the chase aircraft.

Familiarizes the student with differences in photo, safety, and area chase. Students will practice roles and responsibilities for a representative variety of chase maneuvers from both the test aircraft and the chase aircraft.
MIB for RPA student pilots to understand and apply procedures and techniques for 2-ship maneuvering and photo/safety chase for UAV chase missions.
RPA student pilots understand and apply procedures and techniques for 2-ship maneuvering and photo/safety chase for UAV chase missions.
Optional opportunity for student pilots to fly as target for C-12 photo/safety chase missions.

modules.moduleName: Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)

tlos: Explain the basic vocabulary of the various phases of an aerospace test program, from program initiation through final reporting.
Explain the expected actions within the test community at different points in a test program, including the day-to-day responsibilities of an aerospace test professional.
Demonstrate effective and efficient planning and execution of a successful test.

events.description: Introduces students to the basic components involved with planning, executing, analyzing, and reporting during a test program. Provides context for the TF 6000 level of academics and introduces some of the basic vocabulary of aerospace test programs.

Introduces students to the responsibilities of a tester, provides the context to help them understand where developmental testing fits into the greater acquisition enterprise, and introduces them to the processes which support test planning and execution.

Introduces students to the basic format and development strategy for Air Force Test Center (AFTC)-style test plans. Explains the construct of specific and achievable objectives and details the sub-sections that are hallmarks of an executable plan.

A ten-minute group presentation that will take place a few days after the Test Planning Lecture. Students will analyze a provided test vignette, identify requirements and test objectives, develop a test methodology, identify a risk, and communicate their plan.

Provides students with the basics of program management as applied to aerospace test and discusses the theory and application of theory of constraints (TOC) at the Air Force Test Center. TOC is the method used to schedule projects within the constraints of available resources.

Describes ORM and its objectives, the difference between Deliberate and Real-Time Risk Management, the basic ORM 5 Step Process, and the ORM RTRM/ABCD Model. Provides students with common lexicon and methodology to address ORM in a Test and Evaluation context.

Introduces students to the combat test framework which is intended to obtain combat realistic data for a system under test. Students will learn to leverage system decomposition, the integrated evaluation framework, and realistic threat-based combat scenarios to develop test events that produce combat relevant data.

Introduces students to formal Operational Test (OT) in the context of fielding new capabilities. An overview of the US laws governing OT and independent operational assessments. OT objectives and test considerations compared with Developmental Test (DT). Key OT terminology such as Effectiveness and Suitability, OT organizations and considerations for integrated testing.

Currently in development, conducted as a seminar. Instructors and guest speakers will lead the discussion how Integrated Test can be implemented to include lessons learned. Guests from Delta 12 will assist in describing the current vision for Integrated test within the Space T&E community.

prerequisites.name: Test and Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP) Development

tlos: Explain the expected actions within the test community at different points in a test program, including the day-to-day responsibilities of an aerospace test professional.
Demonstrate effective and efficient planning and execution of a successful test.

events.description: Introduces students to the fundamentals of test control and conduct and familiarizes them with content of AFTCI 99-105, Test Control and Conduct. Emphasizes elements required for safe and efficient test control and conduct including required personnel, mission preparation, test card generation, communications plans, execution techniques, and post-test debrief.

Introduces students to the fundamentals of test control and conduct. Covers required personnel, mission preparation, test card generation, communications plans, execution techniques, and post-test debrief.
Introduces students to Time Safety Margin (TSM) calculations, demonstrates how TSM can be utilized for safe test execution, and equips the students to be able to plan their own TSMs.

events.eventName: Control Room Test Conduct Primer MIB
events.description: Presents a flight test scenario composed of various flying qualities and mission system test points. System requirements, test objectives, and measures of performance from the provided test plan are presented.

A mock flight test is conducted from the control room. Students are familiarized with the radio procedures and control room environment and are given the opportunity to practice control of a test mission to include basic test point pacing, data monitoring, and maneuver feedback.

events.eventName: F-16/Control Room Test Conduct Intro MIB
events.description: MIB for utilizing flight test techniques derived from propulsion testing. Introduces methods for conducting a test from a remote telemetry control room.

prerequisites.name: Control Room Test Conduct Primer MIB

events.description: Students are introduced to the methods for conducting a test from a remote telemetry control room. Builds on the control room resources from CF 6621C and concepts from TF 6221C. Increases complexity with active test control and real-world fog and friction of actual flight test. ABM/FTE execute, CSO/P/RPA observe.

prerequisites.name: Control Room Test Conduct Primer MIB

events.eventName: F-16/Control Room Test Conduct Intro Flight
events.description: Optional flight for CSO/P/RPA during F-16/Control Room Test Conduct Intro event.

prerequisites.name: Control Room Test Conduct Primer MIB

events.description: STC version of the F-16/Control Room Test Conduct Intro, utilizing simulator-based control room environment.

prerequisites.name: Control Room Test Conduct Primer MIB

events.eventName: C-12 Airborne Test Conduct Intro MIB
events.description: Gives students information on test conduct techniques, test team coordination, and in-flight data collection. Highlights mission events for a C-12 Airborne Test Conduct Intro flight.

prerequisites.name: Control Room Test Conduct Primer MIB

events.description: Introduces students to directing a test mission as a participating entity not in control of the vehicle flightpath. Instructor will demonstrate test conduct techniques for a ride-along test collecting data from systems independent of the C-12 during a concurrent flight test of C-12 performance FTTs.

prerequisites.name: Control Room Test Conduct Primer MIB

events.eventName: C-12 Intermediate Airborne Test Conduct MIB
events.description: MIB for C-12 based event where students will work in pairs to plan, execute, and debrief a successful test.

prerequisites.name: C-12 Airborne Test Conduct Intro MIB

events.description: Students will work in pairs to plan, execute, and debrief a successful test. Students will be responsible for all aspects of the test, from mission profile development and flight card generation to timeline adherence and real-time decision-making during execution.

prerequisites.name: C-12 Airborne Test Conduct Intro MIB
Data Group Level Accel/Turn Perf Data Flights 2

events.description: Describes the four basic forces of flight, Bernoulli's Theory and Newton's Laws, how a wing makes lift, critical angle of attack and stalls, how center of gravity affects aircraft stability, and why an aircraft turns.

Describes the basic concepts of a pitot-static system and associated instruments, common sources of error in pitot-static measurements, error sources to data accuracy, and the relationship of data accuracy to test and evaluation results.
Provides students with background and lessons learned in developing and executing the low L/D profile in the T-38 aircraft and an opportunity to interact with an experienced T&E professional.
Prepares the class to execute a limited scope glider test. Includes a risk assessment workshop (1.5 hr.) and Test Plan review (1.5 hr.).
Students execute a limited scope glider test, analyze the results and report. Student teams operate in an unfamiliar environment under significant time constraints.
Written report on glider low L/D exercise results and analysis.

events.eventName: Glider Low Lift/Drag (L/D) Follow-Up Exercise MIB
events.description: MIB for the follow-up glider low L/D exercise.
Follow-up glider exercise exposing the student to test conduct in a unique environment while exploring additional concepts.

events.eventName: Glider Low Lift/Drag (L/D) Follow-Up Exercise Report
events.description: Report on follow-up glider low L/D exercise.
T-38 flight executing low L/D profile.

events.eventName: Secure Flight Test / Special Topics Lecture
events.description: Discusses test security considerations and presents some useful techniques for dealing with sensitive tests. US Only.

tlos: The students will apply statistical techniques.

events.description: Teaches students how to use statistical techniques to extract information from test data. Covers variability, random sampling, designed experiments, mechanistic versus empirical models, enumerative versus analytic studies, sample mean and variance versus population mean and variance. Also covers probability distributions, hypothesis tests, linear regression, confidence intervals, and prediction intervals.
Oral report on applying statistical techniques to test problems.

Teaches students how to apply statistical and constrained optimization methods to designing statistically defensible tests while meeting programmatic constraints such as test hours, instrumentation costs, and total budget. The general concept of Design of Experiments (DOE) is derived using optimization theory.

events.eventName: Statistics Based Test Design Oral Report
events.description: Oral report on applying statistical and constrained optimization methods to test design.

Covers techniques for presenting data in a variety of media to communicate a clear message to the intended audience. Methods of presenting data in a visible, clear, and convincing manner are covered along with common presentation pitfalls.

tlos: TLO 1. Create the applicable data products to answer test objectives for a given system under test.
TLO 2. Create data-driven findings of a system under test to various stakeholders at the appropriate level.

events.description: This block introduces students to the concept of test reporting; specifically, why they write reports and where reports fit into the overall test process. It introduces the various types of reports in the test world and lists the reports that students will produce during the curriculum.

This block introduces students to the concept of test reporting; specifically, why they write reports and where reports fit into the overall test process. It introduces the various types of reports in the test world and lists the reports that students will produce during the curriculum.

This block introduces students to deficiency reports, specifically what they are, who can submit them, how they are written, and how they are handled by the test organization, the program office, AFMC, and the contractor once they are written.
This block introduces techniques for reporting on test data in an oral presentation. Common PowerPoint tips and pitfalls are discussed.

tlos: TLO 1. The students will recall critical elements of special test foundations topics.

events.description: This block introduces students to space acquisition/test and identifies the similarities and differences between space and air vehicle test. The student is introduced to the space environment, systems, missions, organizations, acquisition, and test. The block includes a discussion of space policy guidance and specific discussions of space developmental and operational test, including examples and real-world vignettes.

events.eventName: US Only Space Acquisition and Test Lecture
events.description: This block also introduces classified space systems with a discussion of real-world acquisition and test considerations for such systems.

This is a two day-long guided exercise provided by the Air Force Joint Test Program Office. The students will exercise the process that rapidly provides non-materiel solutions to identified operational deficiencies. The course will review operational test definitions such as Critical Operational Issues (COI), System Under Test (SUT), and Tactics, Techniques and Procedures (TTP), and Measures of Effectiveness/Suitability (MOE/MOS). The students will be posed with operational deficiencies and will generate a COI, and associated MOEs/MOSs. The students will develop an execution/data plan for a test event of a non-materiel solution (TTP). They will analyze the results and report on the findings.
This block teaches students why all-weather testing is done, what the major objectives are, where these tests are conducted, and how to plan and execute an all-weather test.

tlos: TLO 1. The student will be able to differentiate between a variety of aircraft/spacecraft performance, flying qualities, and systems characteristics as their experiences broaden by flying multiple platforms.

TLO 2. The student will prioritize collection of appropriate, mission-relevant data which will answer evaluation objectives during planning and execution of single-sortie qualitative evaluations in unfamiliar platforms.
TLO 3. The student will relate qualitative evaluation results to system requirements and the platforms' intended mission in a limited scope written report.

events.description: Students are introduced to the purpose of qualitative evaluations. Techniques are presented to help the students make the most of every qual eval, and specific safety considerations are introduced.

Since most of the qual eval instructor pilots will be contractors, students are reminded of the sensitivities faced when dealing with contractors and are instructed on the proper way to interface with them.
For each of the three graded qual eval flights, an MIB will introduce the aircraft and the mission for which it will be evaluated.
The contractor will provide a ground school covering the major systems of the subject aircraft as well as any required briefing items and aircraft handling techniques.
Following the ground school, students will fly the first graded qualitative evaluation flight.
Following each qual eval flight, students will write a Qual Eval report documenting their flight and evaluating the aircraft for use in a particular mission.
This is the second graded qual eval.
This is the second graded qual eval.
This is the second graded qual eval.
This is the second graded qual eval.
This is the third graded qual eval.
This is the third graded qual eval.
This is the third graded qual eval.
This is the third graded qual eval.

tlos: TLO 1. The student will be able to differentiate between a variety of aircraft/spacecraft performance, flying qualities, and systems characteristics as their experiences broaden by flying multiple platforms.

events.description: Every aircraft brought in will have a short MIB highlighting the aircraft and the mission for which it should be evaluated.

events.eventName: Experience Broadening Qual Eval Ground School 1-6
events.description: Additional unique aircraft will be brought in and flown to expand the students' experience base. The contractor will provide the appropriate ground school. The single digit indicates required prerequisites for the ground school and flights (e.g., 7210G is required before 7220F, 7211G is required before 7221F).
Additional unique aircraft will be brought in and flown to expand the students' experience base. No report is required following the flight.

events.eventName: C-17 Experience Broadening Qual Eval Ground School
events.description: The ground school will cover the required areas of operation for students to evaluate the C-17 in groups.

events.eventName: C-17 Experience Broadening Qual Eval Flight
events.description: There is no reporting requirement for this flight.

Utilizing high-fidelity simulators, students will be exposed to two modern heavy aircraft of different designs to compare different design philosophies. In addition to experiencing test operations in a large aircraft, students will perform high-risk ground and flight test FTTs and large aircraft FTTs that cannot be performed inflight at TPS due to safety and aircraft availability considerations.

events.eventName: Navy TPS Fixed Wing Qual Eval Ground School
events.description: During a visit to the US Navy Test Pilot School (USNTPS), experience broadening non-graded qual evals may include an F/A-18 out of control flight demonstration (when available), a single-engine tail dragger for pilots, and a T-6B for non-pilots.

events.eventName: Navy TPS Fixed Wing Qual Eval Flight
events.description: During a visit to the US Navy Test Pilot School (USNTPS), experience broadening non-graded qual evals may include an F/A-18 out of control flight demonstration (when available), a single-engine tail dragger for pilots, and a T-6B for non-pilots.

tlos: TLO 1. Analyze performance, flying qualities, and systems characteristics for rotary wing aircraft.

events.eventName: Intro to Rotary Wing Flight MIB
events.description: This MIB will outline the learning objectives of having USAF TPS students fly rotary wing aircraft and will provide basic warnings and limitations students can expect.

events.eventName: Intro to Rotary Wing Ground School
events.description: The ground school will cover basic rotary wing flight dynamics, and the different controls involved in flying helicopters to prepare students for their introductory flight.
Introductory rotary wing flight for USAF TPS students.

events.eventName: Advanced Rotary Wing Qual Eval Ground School
events.description: Students will be exposed to rotary wing flight test techniques during their visit to USNTPS (or Eglin AFB). All related ground school and academics will take place at the host location.

prerequisites.name: Intro to Rotary Wing Flight MIB

events.eventName: Advanced Rotary Wing Qual Eval Flight
events.description: This experience broadening non-graded qual eval will expose the students to unique rotary wing flight characteristics and flight test techniques.

prerequisites.name: Intro to Rotary Wing Flight MIB

tlos: TLO 1. The students will understand the relationship of various organizations.

events.description: Students will observe a single real-world flight-test mission/control room to provide them with a better perspective of real-world control room operations. The event will also help students better visualize the kind of roles and responsibilities that they may have post-graduation.

prerequisites.name: Secure Flight Test - Special Topics Lecture
prerequisites.originalNotes: US Only; req'd for US Only Control Rooms

tlos: TLO 1. The student will be able to differentiate between a variety of aircraft/spacecraft performance, flying qualities, and systems characteristics as their experiences broaden by flying multiple platforms.

events.description: The student team will develop a test plan to evaluate an unfamiliar aircraft in a scenario that the students develop.

Students will deconstruct the scenario into relevant qual eval test objectives and appropriate flight test techniques for obtaining the qualitative and quantitative data necessary to inform the test objectives. In addition to the test plan, test cards will also be submitted for staff monitor review.
The student will fly a single qual eval sortie at the capstone host location to collect data for the report.

Upon returning from their capstone qual eval, students will present their findings in a brief oral report to the school. Students in the junior class observe the oral report. This event provides students the opportunity to demonstrate their ability to plan, execute, and deliver an oral report on a qualitative evaluation of an unfamiliar aircraft.

tlos: TLO 1. The student will be able to differentiate between a variety of aircraft/spacecraft performance, flying qualities, and systems characteristics as their experiences broaden by flying multiple platforms.

events.description: This lesson is a precursor to the TF 5230M Human Systems Evaluation MIB. It provides familiarization with the WGS Technical Orders and other engineering documentation students will be interacting with during the exercise.

tlos: TLO 1. The student test team will construct a real-world test program and collaborate with key stakeholders through the full lifecycle - from test concept through final report and presentation of the results.
TLO 2. The student test team will design a test to answer a customer's needs and modify their approach throughout the lifecycle of the program to achieve test program objectives.

events.description: Students will be presented with their assigned TMP groups and tasked to self-select who their student Program Manager will be for their group. In addition to expectations and timelines for the TMP process, a basic overview of the 412 Test Wing Program Management process will be covered.

Each student team will meet with the USAF TPS Program Managers, TMP Director, and their Staff Advisors to discuss what their specific programmatic requirements and resources are. Students will be instructed to draft a 412 TW Test Concept Document (TCD).

The A5 Program Manager will formally begin the 412 TW PID/SOC process. During the Core Team Meeting, the student teams will present their TCDs to the respective organizations, allowing the organizations an opportunity to either concur or non-concur to the support being requested.

The first TPWG will focus on defining their general test objectives (GTOs), specific test objectives (STOs), measures of performance (MOPs), and any outstanding technical planning discussions that will feed directly into their test plan development.

The TMP Director, Staff Advisors, and assigned Unit Test Safety Officer, will review the students' updated draft test and safety plans. The focus will be on ensuring the team's test plan objectives, methodologies, and data analysis plans are well constructed.

events.eventName: TMP Test Plan and Safety Package
events.description: Graded test and safety plans, submitted to the TPS led Technical Review Board, and the 412 TW SET led Safety Review Board.

events.eventName: TMP Technical Review Board/Safety Review Board
events.description: The purpose of the Technical Review Board (TRB) is to have the student teams present their TMP test plans to the USAF TPS Technical Director. The Safety Review Board (SRB) is to provide a safety review and risk assessment of the finalized students' test safety plan.
Upon completion of both the TRB and SRB, the students will document and correct any action items, prior to proceeding with their request for test execution approval.

tlos: TLO 1. The student test team will execute their TMP data flights/test events, in accordance with their approved test and safety plans.

events.description: The purpose of the test missions is to collect data in accordance with the test plan and safety package to answer the customer's questions.

tlos: TLO 1. The student teams will brief their peers and TPS Staff on what their planned TMP test objectives, test execution schedule, and expected test results are.

TLO 2. The student teams will inform their peers and TPS Staff on the status of their completed test execution, preliminary data results, and any constraints or limitations that occurred during execution.
TLO 3. The student teams will author a technical information memorandum, that identifies the data collected, their analysis methods, and presents their data driven conclusions and recommendations.

events.description: The purpose of the first Program Assessment Review is for each student TMP team to present to their peers and TPS staff, their planned test objectives, test methodology, test schedule, and expected test results.

prerequisites.name: TMP Technical Review Board/Safety Review Board

events.description: The purpose of this meeting is to brief students on the reporting expectations for their TMP, instruct them on how to write a TPS technical information memorandum (TIM), and to highlight the differences between the USAF TPS TMP TIM versus the 412 TW Technical Report.

prerequisites.name: TMP Technical Review Board/Safety Review Board

events.description: The second Program Assessment Review is intended to have the student teams brief their peers and TPS Staff, on what actually occurred during their two-week TMP execution window, including preliminary status of data analysis and any constraints or limitations.

prerequisites.name: TMP Technical Review Board/Safety Review Board

events.description: A post-execution, one-hour meeting with the student teams, TMP Director, staff project representative, and appropriate operations/technical experts to discuss data collected, analysis progress and way forward, and the anticipated scope of results and conclusions.

prerequisites.name: TMP Technical Review Board/Safety Review Board

events.description: The Report Coordination Meeting is a graded event for TMPs, equivalent to the 412 TW murder board. The purpose is to review the students' technical information memorandums by conducting a line-by-line review.

prerequisites.name: TMP Technical Review Board/Safety Review Board

events.description: The final signed and approved TMP TIM, is an official USAF TPS document that will be submitted to the TMP customers. Depending on the distribution statement and classification of the TIM, they will be submitted for Public Release Approval and stored in the Defense Technical Information Center and/or 412 TW Technical Library.

prerequisites.name: TMP Technical Review Board/Safety Review Board

events.description: The TMP final oral report, is a graded 45-minute presentation by each of the student teams to brief their peers, AFTC, STARCOM, 412 TW, Delta 12, and Senior Civilian Leadership, and key stakeholders on their TMP results.

prerequisites.name: TMP Technical Review Board/Safety Review Board

tlos: TLO 1. The students will synthesize knowledge across the curriculum.

events.description: MIB for the comprehensive written exam.

Students will be tested on major concepts from the USAF Test Pilot School curriculum: domain sciences, and mission systems. The comprehensive written exam will be conducted once the foundational classroom academics have been presented in each of the sciences.

prerequisites.name: Advanced Kalman Filter and Relative Navigation
Stall, Departure and Spin Testing Exam
First Flight and Envelope Expansion Exam
Performance Phase Final Test Oral Report
Systems Engineering for Testers Project Written Report

events.description: MIB for the comprehensive oral exam.

Students will be evaluated on their ability to analyze a test scenario, identify proper objectives, identify primary safety/security/technical/program risks, demonstrate understanding of fundamental engineering principles, translate objectives into test techniques, demonstrate proper Test Judgement, and effectively communicate and defend the test program. Students will develop a plan in 40 minutes for evaluating a system and present to a review board of five USAF TPS instructors or graduates.

prerequisites.name: T-38 Flying Qualities Practical Control Room
F-16 Systems Practical Exam Control Room
